feed.title: Post-Quantum BIP-86 Recovery via zk-STARK Proof of BIP-32 Seed Knowledge

entry.title: Post-Quantum BIP-86 Recovery via zk-STARK Proof of BIP-32 Seed Knowledge
entry.summary:
The discussion revolves around the potential application of a single STARK to authenticate a spender through proof of knowing an xpriv that derives specific keys within a Bitcoin transaction. This ability would enable any verifier to derive private scan/spend keys and replicate the ECDH process with BIP352-specific tweaks, ultimately proving the authenticity of the spender by reproducing the private key of the SP taproot output being spent. This method could potentially encompass all UTXO in a silent payment wallet due to the commonality of scan/spend keys across derived output-specific addresses.
However, there are security concerns with this approach compared to conventional BIP32 xpriv ownership proofs. Typically, xpubs and xprivs are treated with high confidentiality as xpubs can compromise privacy and xprivs can jeopardize security. Contrastingly, hashed address pubkeys, which are commonly shared on-chain, do not receive the same level of protection. The proposal suggests that employing zero-knowledge proofs for spending could expose coins to vulnerabilities, especially if attackers know the corresponding `k*G` values critical for CRQC attacks. This risk is further magnified when spenders reveal their account-level xpriv during transactions, potentially allowing attackers to forge proofs and facilitate double-spending or Replace-by-Fee (RBF) attacks.
To address these issues, a mutually exclusive system was proposed where verifiers would accept a BIP32 xpriv proof only if the address is listed under "exposed pubkey," while a hashed-address proof would be accepted if the address is not on this list. Implementing both proofs safely remains challenging due to potential overlaps between non-BIP32 users and those with non-exposed pubkeys — a demographic difficult to quantify without detailed research on UTXOs predating BIP32's introduction in 2012. This area presents an opportunity for valuable research for anyone equipped with the time and resources to analyze historical P2PKH UTXOs using a working node.
